cues.x: $100M Offers, by Alex Hormozi.
Let me start with a promise, because that is exactly what this book is about.

By the time we finish this walkthrough, you will understand how to build something Alex Hormozi calls a Grand Slam Offer, an offer so good that the people you want to serve feel almost stupid saying no to it.
Not a slightly better pitch.
Not a cleverer headline.
A fundamentally different thing that people cannot easily compare to anything else, so they stop asking how much it costs and start asking how fast they can start.

If you sell anything at all, a service, a product, coaching, software, your own time, this is one of the most practical books ever written on why some businesses print money while others, selling roughly the same thing, quietly starve.
So settle in, keep your eyes on the road, and let me walk you through the whole thing.
First, who is Alex Hormozi and why should you trust him on this.
His story is the spine of the book, so let me tell it, because it makes everything that follows land harder.
Hormozi did not start rich.
He started as a gym owner.
And not a thriving one.
At his lowest point he was down to about a thousand dollars to his name, staring at the very real possibility that the whole thing collapses.
He had put in the sweat, he had the equipment, he had clients walking in the door, and he still could not make the math work.
That is the part people forget.
You can work incredibly hard and still lose, if the underlying offer is weak.
What turned it around was not working harder.
It was changing what he was selling and how he was packaging it.
He rebuilt the offer.
And when the offer got strong enough, everything downstream got easier.
Sales got easier.
Marketing got easier.
Fulfillment got easier, because clients who paid a real price showed up and did the work.
From that turnaround he built a company called Gym Launch, which took the offer he had figured out and taught it to other gym owners, and then licensed it, and it exploded.
From there he went on to build a portfolio company called Acquisition.com, doing enormous numbers, the kind of numbers that get measured in the hundreds of millions of dollars of value.
And through every single one of those businesses, across totally different industries, he says the same lever was underneath all of it.
The offer.
Not the logo, not the funnel software, not some secret traffic hack.
The offer.
The thing you actually promise, priced and packaged in a way that makes the buying decision feel obvious.
That is the whole thesis.
Get the offer right and you can be mediocre at ten other things and still win.
Get the offer wrong and you can be brilliant at everything else and still grind yourself into the ground.
Now here is the first hard truth, and it is going to sting a little if you are in love with your product.
The market matters more than the offer.
And the offer matters more than persuasion.
Hormozi ranks them in that order on purpose.
You could have the most beautifully constructed offer, delivered by the smoothest salesperson alive, and if you are pointing it at a bad market, you lose.
He borrows a famous lesson from an old-school direct-response legend named Gary Halbert to make this stick, and it is worth telling slowly because once you hear it you never unhear it.
Halbert used to ask a room full of ambitious people a question.
Imagine you want to open a hamburger stand, and you are going to compete against me.
What advantage would you want.
People would shout out answers.
I want the best beef.
I want a secret sauce.
I want the best location.
I want the lowest prices.
I want the most loyal staff.
And Halbert would let them go, list all their advantages, and then he would say, fine, you can have all of them.
Every single one.
I only want one advantage, and with it I will beat you every time.
I want a starving crowd.
That is the whole thing.
If you are standing in front of a crowd of people who are absolutely starving, desperate for what you sell, you can fumble half the details and still win.
If you are standing in front of people who are full, who do not care, who have no urgency, then your perfect beef and your perfect sauce and your perfect price mean nothing.
Demand is the game.
Find the starving crowd first.
So how do you know a market is a starving crowd.
Hormozi gives you four traits to check, and you can hold them in your head easily.
First, the market has real, painful, pressing pain.
Not a mild preference.
Actual pain that keeps people up at night, the kind they will pay to make stop.
Second, the market has buying power.
It is not enough for people to want it desperately, they have to be able to pay.
A crowd of broke people who want your thing is not a business, it is a charity.
Third, the market is easy to target.
You can actually find these people, reach them, put your message in front of them without hunting for a needle in a haystack.
Are there lists, groups, channels, places where they gather.
Fourth, the market is growing.
A rising tide lifts you.
Selling into a shrinking, dying market means you are swimming against the current forever, even if you do everything right.
Real pain, buying power, easy to target, and growing.
Run any market you are considering through those four and you will kill a lot of bad ideas before they kill you.
Then Hormozi points at the three markets that never go away, the ones humans have paid for since the beginning and will keep paying for until the end.
Health, wealth, and relationships.
Or put more bluntly, people want to be healthier, they want to be richer, and they want to be more loved and connected.
Every durable business is really selling one of those three, or a bridge between them.
Losing weight is health.
Making more money in your business is wealth.
Saving your marriage, finding a partner, being a better parent, that is relationships.
If your offer does not ultimately connect to one of those three deep human drivers, you have to ask whether the desire underneath it is strong enough to build on.
But here is the twist that makes people rich, and it is the phrase, riches in the niches.
Do not sell to everyone.
Narrow down.
The narrower and more specific your niche, the more you can charge, and the easier everything gets.
Hormozi gives the intuition like this.
Imagine you sell a general time management course.
It is fifty dollars, maybe, because everyone and their brother has a time management course, and the buyer has no reason to believe yours is special for them.
Now imagine you take that exact same material and you re-aim it.
A time management course for female executives who have young children.
Suddenly a specific woman hears that and thinks, that is for me, that person understands my exact life.
She will pay far more than fifty dollars, because it is not a commodity anymore, it is a solution to her particular problem.
Same core content.
Narrower niche.
Multiply the price.
And you can go narrower still.
Not just health, but back pain.
Not just back pain, but back pain for new mothers.
The more specific the person and the problem, the higher the perceived relevance, the higher the price, and the lower the competition.
That is riches in the niches.
Now that we have the market, let me hit the enemy you are actually fighting, because most businesses do not lose to a competitor, they lose to a concept.
That concept is commoditization.
A commodity is anything the buyer can comparison shop, anything where the only real difference in their mind is price.

If a customer can line you up next to three other options and the main lever they are pulling is which one is cheapest, you are a commodity, and you are trapped in what Hormozi calls a race to the bottom.
In a race to the bottom, everybody keeps cutting price to win the deal, and margins get thinner and thinner until nobody is making money and everybody is exhausted.
The way out is differentiation, and not the weak kind where you just claim to be a little better.
The goal is to become a category of one.
Something so distinct in what it promises and how it is packaged that the buyer literally cannot line you up next to anyone else, because there is no one else offering that specific thing.
When you cannot be compared, you cannot be comparison shopped, and when you cannot be comparison shopped, the conversation stops being about price.
That is the entire reason the Grand Slam Offer exists.
It is a mechanism for escaping commoditization.
Which brings us to one of the most counterintuitive arguments in the book, and I want you to really sit with this one, because it is the opposite of what scared business owners do.
Hormozi says charge more.
Not a little more.
A lot more.
When things get hard, the instinct is to lower your price to get more customers in the door.
He says that instinct is a trap, and he explains it with two cycles that pull in opposite directions.
The first is the vicious cycle, the death spiral, and it starts with a low price.
You price low to win customers.
Low price means thin margins, so you have very little money left over per sale.
With thin margins you cannot afford to give a great experience.
You cannot afford great support, great onboarding, great extras, great people, because there is nothing left to spend.
A worse experience gets worse results for the client, and the client, who did not pay much anyway, is not very committed, so they do not show up and do the work, which also gets them worse results.
Worse results mean worse word of mouth, more refunds, more complaints, and so to keep sales coming you discount even further.
Lower price, thinner margins, worse experience, worse results, more discounting.
Round and round, down and down.
That is where the broke gym owner version of Hormozi was living.
The second is the virtuous cycle, and it runs the opposite direction, starting from a premium price.
You charge a premium.
A premium price means fat margins, real money left over on every sale.

With fat margins you can afford an incredible experience, great people, great delivery, great extras, and you can also afford to spend more to acquire each customer, which means you can outbid everyone for attention.
On top of that, and this is the subtle part, a client who paid a premium is far more committed.
They have skin in the game.
They paid real money so they actually show up, they do the work, they take it seriously, and they get better results.
Better results plus a better experience means better word of mouth, more referrals, higher retention, and the ability to charge even more next time.
Premium price, fat margins, great experience and great acquisition, plus a committed client who gets real results.
Round and round, up and up.
Same business, same effort, completely different trajectory, and the only thing you changed at the start was the price.
And if that still feels like a trick, Hormozi points to something almost unbelievable from the research on placebos.
In studies, a placebo, a sugar pill with no active medicine in it at all, relieved more pain when the patient was told it was expensive than when they were told it was cheap.
Let that land.
The identical pill, zero difference in the chemistry, produced a stronger real effect just because the price was higher.
Price is not only what you pay.
Price is a signal that changes how much the buyer values the thing, how seriously they take it, and, astonishingly, how well it actually works for them.
So when you charge more, you are not just making more money.
In many cases you are literally making your product work better for your customer, because they believe in it more and they commit to it more.
Charging more can be the more ethical choice, because your clients get better outcomes.
Okay.
We have the market, we have the case for premium price, and we have the enemy, commoditization.
Now we get to the engine at the center of the entire book.
The Value Equation.
This is the thing I most want you to be able to picture in your mind while you drive, so let me build it slowly, piece by piece, like a scale or a fraction.
Picture a fraction.
A line across the middle, some things stacked on top, some things stacked on the bottom.
The whole fraction represents the value your customer perceives, how much they want to say yes.
On top of the line, above it, sit two things that make value go up.
On the bottom of the line, below it, sit two things that make value go down.
Your entire job in crafting an offer is to make the top as big as possible and the bottom as small as possible.
On top, the first thing is the Dream Outcome.
This is the result the customer actually wants, the reason they are really buying.
Nobody wants a gym membership, they want to look good with their shirt off and feel confident at the beach.
Nobody wants a drill, they want the hole, and really the shelf on the wall, and really the feeling of a finished, tidy home.
The dream outcome is the big, emotional, specific end state they are dreaming about.
The bigger and more vivid and more desirable that dream is in their mind, the higher the value goes.
So one lever you pull is painting and delivering a bigger, clearer dream.
On top, the second thing is the Perceived Likelihood of Achievement.
This is the customer's gut belief that they, specifically they, will actually get that dream outcome if they buy from you.
It does not matter how gorgeous the dream is if they secretly do not believe it will work for them.
We have all seen the diet that promises the beach body but we roll our eyes because we do not believe it will work, not for us, not with our history of failed diets.
So the second lever is proof and believability.
Testimonials, case studies, guarantees, demonstrations, credentials, a mechanism that makes sense, anything that raises their gut confidence that this will really work.
Dream outcome on top, perceived likelihood of achievement on top.
Push both of those up and value rises.
Now the bottom of the fraction, the two things that drag value down, and here, Hormozi says, is where the real magic is usually hiding, because most people obsess over the top and ignore the bottom.
On the bottom, the first thing is Time Delay.
How long does the customer have to wait between paying you and getting the result they want.
The longer the wait, the lower the value, because humans are impatient and a far-off reward feels weaker than a near one.
If you can deliver the result faster, or even deliver a meaningful quick win right away while the big result is still cooking, value goes up.
People will pay dramatically more to get there sooner.
Speed sells.
On the bottom, the second thing is Effort and Sacrifice.
How much work, pain, hassle, learning, discomfort, and giving-up does the customer have to endure to get the result.
Every ounce of effort they have to put in, every sacrifice they have to make, every hard thing they have to learn or do or give up, drags the value down.

If losing weight requires them to cook every meal, count every calorie, and go to the gym at five in the morning, that is enormous effort and sacrifice, and it crushes the perceived value even if the dream is huge.
If you can strip that effort out, do it for them, make it easy, remove the sacrifice, value shoots up.
So there is your fraction.
On top, the Dream Outcome and the Perceived Likelihood of Achievement, the things you want as large as possible.
On the bottom, the Time Delay and the Effort and Sacrifice, the things you want as small as possible, ideally driving them toward zero.
Value equals the dream and the belief, divided by the wait and the work.
And here is the insight that separates good offers from world-class ones.
The top two, the dream outcome and the perceived likelihood, are limited.
There is only so big you can make the dream before it becomes unbelievable, and there is only so high you can push believability.
But the bottom two, time delay and effort, can be pushed all the way down toward zero.
And when the denominator of a fraction goes toward zero, the value of the whole fraction goes toward infinity.
Do you feel that.
As time delay and effort approach nothing, perceived value approaches everything.
That is the mathematical soul of the Grand Slam Offer.
The magic is not mostly in promising a bigger dream.
The magic is in collapsing the time and erasing the effort.
This is exactly why, Hormozi says, done-for-you beats done-with-you, which beats do-it-yourself.
Think about those three ways to sell the same result.
Do-it-yourself is you hand the customer a course or a tool and they do all the work themselves.
Maximum effort on them, so the value is lowest and you can charge the least.
Done-with-you is you coach them, you do it alongside them, you guide them.
Less effort on them, more value, you can charge more.
Done-for-you is you just do the whole thing for them and hand them the result.
Almost zero effort on them, fastest path to the outcome, highest value, and you can charge the most by far.
Most people instinctively build the cheap do-it-yourself version because it is easy for them to make.
The money is in moving up that ladder, taking the effort and the time off the customer's plate and onto yours, and charging accordingly.
Whenever you are stuck on how to make an offer more valuable, ask, how can I make this faster for them, and how can I make this require less of them.
Those two questions are where fortunes hide.
Alright.
You understand the market, the pricing psychology, and the value equation.
Now let me give you the actual step-by-step process for building the Grand Slam Offer, because this is where it goes from theory to something you can do this week.
Step one.
Start with the dream outcome and get crystal clear on it.
What is the ultimate result your customer wants.
Write it down in their words, the vivid emotional version, not the dry feature version.
Step two, and this is the big one.
List out every single problem and obstacle that stands between the customer and that dream outcome.
Every one.
Brainstorm exhaustively.
What could go wrong.
What do they have to figure out.
What are they afraid of.
What has stopped them before.
What will stop them along the way.

If the dream is losing thirty pounds, the obstacles are things like, I do not know what to eat, I do not have time to cook, I do not know how to work out, I will get bored, I will not stay motivated, I will get injured, I will not know if it is working, my family will not eat healthy food with me, I have tried before and failed and I am scared to fail again.
Get every single obstacle out onto the page.
Be thorough, because each obstacle is not a problem, it is raw material.
Each one is a future piece of your offer.
Step three.
Turn each of those problems into a solution.
Go down your list, one obstacle at a time, and for each one, describe the thing you could provide that makes that specific obstacle disappear.
I do not know what to eat becomes a done-for-you meal plan.
I do not have time to cook becomes pre-portioned meals or a grocery delivery integration or a fifteen-minute recipe pack.
I will not stay motivated becomes weekly accountability check-ins.
I do not know if it is working becomes a progress tracking dashboard and monthly measurements.
I am scared to fail again becomes a guarantee.
Notice what just happened.
You took a long list of the customer's fears and you converted the entire list into a long list of solutions.
Every obstacle now has an antidote.
Frame those solutions as the things you deliver, and phrase them as actions and outcomes, not vague features.
Step four.
Now you have a big pile of possible solutions, and here is where judgment comes in.
You trim and you stack.
Not everything on that list is worth including.
Some solutions cost you a lot to deliver but provide little value to the customer.
Cut those, or minimize them.
Some solutions are cheap or easy for you to deliver but hugely valuable in the customer's eyes.
Keep every one of those and put them front and center.
Hormozi has you think about each potential component on two axes.
How much does it cost me to provide, and how much value does the customer perceive in it.
The dream components are high value to the customer and low cost to you.
A piece of software you built once and can hand to unlimited people at no extra cost, that is high value and low marginal cost, a perfect thing to stack.
Live one-on-one time with you every day, high value but very high cost, so use it sparingly or only at the top tier.
You are curating.
You are assembling the collection of high-value pieces that together attack every obstacle the customer has, while keeping your own delivery costs sane.
Step five.
Stack the value and present it so the total dwarfs the price.
This is the theatrical, and honestly kind of beautiful, part of a Grand Slam Offer.
You do not just say, here is my thing, it costs three thousand dollars.
Instead you lay out every single component you are including, one at a time, and you attach a real, defensible value to each one.
The done-for-you meal plan, that would normally be worth this much.
The weekly accountability coaching, worth this much.
The progress tracking system, worth this much.
The private community, the recipe vault, the guarantee, each one named, each one with its own value.
And you add them all up out loud, so by the time you reach the end, the total stacked value is enormous, far, far larger than what you are going to charge.
Then you reveal the price, and the price sits so far below the value you just stacked that the gap between them becomes the reason to buy.
That gap, the distance between the mountain of value and the price, is the exact place where the feeling lives, the feeling of, wait, I would be an idiot to pass this up.
That is the feel stupid saying no gap, and you engineer it deliberately by stacking real value high above a fair price.
The point is never to trick anyone.
The point is to actually assemble so much genuine value that the price becomes almost irrelevant to the decision.
Now, once you have that stacked, valuable, well-priced offer, you can make it dramatically more powerful with what Hormozi calls the four enhancers.
These are the amplifiers.

The offer can stand on its own, but these four turn up the volume and, importantly, they attack the last thing standing between a person and a purchase, which is inertia, the tendency to say, this looks great, let me think about it, and then never come back.
Let me walk you through all four.
The first enhancer is scarcity.
Scarcity is about limited supply.
It is the fear of missing out on a fixed quantity.
There are only so many spots, only so many units, only so many clients I can take this quarter.
When something is genuinely limited in supply, its perceived value rises and people act now rather than later, because later the thing might be gone.
Hormozi describes several honest ways to build scarcity.
You can limit the total number of clients you will ever take.
You can limit the number of spots available in a given period, say, I only onboard six new clients a month.
You can limit seats in a cohort.
The key word is honest.
The scarcity has to be real.
If you say only ten spots and then sell an eleventh, you have taught your market that your word means nothing, and that poisons everything.
Real, enforced limits protect your delivery quality and create genuine urgency at the same time.
The second enhancer is urgency.
If scarcity is about limited supply, urgency is about limited time.
It is a real deadline.
The offer, or this price, or these bonuses, go away at a specific moment.
A cohort that starts on a fixed date.
A pricing window that closes Friday.
A seasonal or event-based deadline that genuinely exists.
Urgency works because a decision with no deadline is a decision that gets postponed forever, and a postponed decision is usually a lost sale, even when the person really wanted to buy.
A real deadline gives them a reason to decide now instead of someday.
And again, the deadline has to be real.
Fake countdown timers that reset when you refresh the page train people to distrust you.
If you say the price goes up Friday, the price goes up Friday.
When urgency is honest, it is a gift to the buyer, because it forces the decision they were going to keep avoiding to their own detriment.
The third enhancer is bonuses, and this one contains a piece of pricing wisdom that is worth the price of the whole book.
When a customer hesitates on price, your instinct is to discount.
Hormozi says, do not discount, add bonuses instead.
Here is why.

If your thing costs a thousand dollars and you knock off two hundred, you have just permanently told the customer your thing was never really worth a thousand, you have trained them to always ask for a discount, and you have thrown away two hundred dollars of margin.

But if instead of discounting you add a bonus worth several hundred dollars to them, something that may cost you very little to provide, you have increased the perceived value of the deal without lowering your price and without cheapening your core offer.
You keep your price integrity and you make the pile bigger.
And the way you present bonuses matters enormously.
Each bonus should be a distinct, named item, described with its own benefit, and given its own dollar value, just like the components in your stack.
Not, and you also get some extra stuff.

Instead, and you also get the Fast Start Toolkit, which gets you your first result in the first seven days, a five hundred dollar value, and the Private Vault of done-for-you templates, a three hundred dollar value, and so on.
Each bonus adds to the mountain, each one solves another little obstacle or fear, and each one widens that gap between value and price.
Bonuses are how you handle price resistance like a professional instead of an amateur.

The fourth enhancer is guarantees, and this is arguably the single most powerful lever of the four, because it directly attacks the biggest thing on top of your value equation, the perceived likelihood of achievement.
A guarantee reverses the risk.
Right now, when a customer considers buying, all the risk sits on their shoulders.
What if it does not work.
What if I waste my money.
What if I fail again.
A guarantee takes that risk off their shoulders and puts it onto yours.
You are saying, if this does not do what I promised, you do not carry the loss, I do.
The moment you say that convincingly, the buyer's fear drops and their confidence rises, and the whole value equation swings in your favor.
Hormozi walks through several flavors of guarantee, and they get bolder as you go.
The simplest is an unconditional guarantee, the basic if you are not happy, you get your money back.
Fine, but weak, because everyone says it.
Stronger is a conditional guarantee, where you promise a specific result if the customer does specific things.
If you follow the program, attend the sessions, and do the work for ninety days and you do not get the result, I will refund you, or I will work with you for free until you do.
The condition is not a trick, it is actually a nudge.
It tells the customer exactly what they need to do to succeed, and it filters for people who will actually do the work, which means better results and fewer refunds.
Then there are the bold, dramatic guarantees, the ones that make people gasp a little.
I will get you your result, or I will pay you.
I will refund you double.
I will fly out and do it myself.
These sound insane, and that is the point, they signal enormous confidence, and confidence is contagious.

There is even what he calls an anti-guarantee, used for certain expensive or bespoke offers, where you explicitly say all sales are final and no refunds, and you frame it in a way that actually screams confidence and seriousness, this is only for people who are truly committed.
Different guarantees fit different businesses.
But here is the counterintuitive number that makes people brave enough to offer wild guarantees.
Hormozi's experience is that a bold guarantee wins you far more sales than it ever costs you in refunds.
Yes, a bigger, crazier guarantee will trigger a few more refund requests, that is true.
But it will trigger so many more sales, from people who were on the fence and needed their risk removed, that the extra revenue dwarfs the extra refunds many times over.
The math almost always favors the braver guarantee.
Most business owners are too scared to offer a strong guarantee because they picture the refunds, and they never see the far larger number of sales they lost by being timid.
So the lesson is, be bold with your guarantee, make it specific, make it about the result the customer actually wants, and trust that the increased sales will vastly outweigh the increased refunds.
A word of care, of course.
Only guarantee what you can influence, deliver honestly, and make the terms clear.
A guarantee built on a lie destroys you.
A guarantee built on genuine confidence in a genuinely good offer is rocket fuel.
There is one more piece before we synthesize, and it is easy to underestimate.
Naming your offer.
Hormozi is clear that what you call your offer materially changes how many people respond to it, because the name is often the very first and sometimes the only thing a prospect reads.
A good name does a few jobs at once.
It points clearly at the dream outcome, so the person instantly knows what they get.
It often includes a timeframe, so they know how fast, which speaks directly to that time delay lever on the bottom of the value equation.
And it wraps everything in an appealing container, a format word that feels exciting and concrete, like a Challenge, or a Blueprint, or a Bootcamp, or an Intensive, or an Accelerator.
Think of the difference between weight loss coaching and something like the Six Week Shred Challenge.
One is a category, forgettable and comparison-shoppable.
The other names the dream, the shred, names the timeframe, six weeks, and wraps it in an appealing container, a Challenge, which sounds like something you join and win.
Same underlying service, wildly different response.
And one more practical note on naming.
Names wear out.

When you run the same offer name in your ads long enough, the market gets tired of seeing it, response drops, and that is your signal to refresh the name and the packaging, keep the substance, put a new label and a new angle on it, and watch response climb again.
The offer is durable.
The wrapper is disposable and should be rotated.
So let me pull the whole thing together now into one clear picture, because we have covered a lot of ground and I want you to be able to hold it as a single shape in your mind as you drive.
You start by finding a starving crowd, because the market beats the offer and the offer beats persuasion.

You look for a market with real pressing pain, with the money to pay, that you can actually reach, and that is growing, and you notice that the deepest, most durable desires live in health, wealth, and relationships.
Then you get rich in the niches, narrowing down to a specific person with a specific problem, because the narrower you go the more you can charge and the less you have to compete.
You refuse to be a commodity, refuse the race to the bottom, and you set out to become a category of one that nobody can comparison shop.

You find the courage to charge a premium, because premium price sets off the virtuous cycle of fat margins, great experience, committed clients, and better results, while cheap prices set off the death spiral, and because, remember the expensive placebo, the price itself changes how well the thing works.
Then you build the value.
You hold the value equation in your head like a fraction.
On top, the dream outcome and the perceived likelihood of achievement, which you push as high as you honestly can with a vivid promise and mountains of proof.

On the bottom, the time delay and the effort and sacrifice, which you drive as close to zero as you possibly can, because that is where value explodes toward the sky, which is exactly why done-for-you beats done-with-you beats do-it-yourself.

You list every problem and obstacle between the customer and the dream, you turn each obstacle into a solution, you trim the pieces that cost you a lot and give little, you stack the pieces that give a lot and cost you little, and you present that stack so the total value towers over the price, opening up the gap where the feeling of, I would be stupid to say no, is born.
And then you turn up the volume with the four enhancers.
Scarcity, limited supply, only so many spots, honestly enforced.
Urgency, limited time, a real deadline that forces the decision people keep avoiding.
Bonuses, added to the pile instead of discounts taken off the price, each one a named item with its own value, so you handle resistance without ever cheapening your core.

And guarantees, reversing the risk from the customer onto you, bold and conditional and specific, tied to the result they actually want, remembering that the wild guarantee wins far more sales than it ever loses to refunds.
Finally you name the offer well, pointing at the dream, naming the timeframe, wrapping it in an appealing container, and you refresh that name whenever the ads start to tire.
Now let me leave you with the concrete actions, the things to actually do, so this is not just a nice thing you listened to but a thing that changes your business.
First, this week, take whatever you currently sell and write down the dream outcome your customer actually wants, in their words, the emotional version, not your feature list.
If you cannot state it in one vivid sentence, that is your first fix.
Second, sit down and brainstorm the complete list of every problem and obstacle between them and that dream.
Do not stop at five.
Push to fifteen or twenty.
The completeness of this list is the raw material for everything else, and most people quit this step too early.

Third, go down that list and convert every obstacle into a solution you could provide, then sort those solutions by two questions, how much value does the customer see in this, and how much does it cost me to deliver.
Circle the high-value, low-cost pieces.
Those are the spine of your new offer.
Cut or minimize the high-cost, low-value ones.
Fourth, attack the bottom of the value equation on purpose.
Ask, how do I get this person their result faster, and how do I take the effort and the hard parts off their plate and onto mine.
Every step you move from do-it-yourself toward done-for-you, you can raise the price and raise the value at the same time.
Fifth, build your stack, assign an honest value to every component, add them up, and set a premium price that still sits far below that stacked value.
Then add your enhancers.
Decide your real, enforceable scarcity.
Set a real deadline for urgency.
Design two or three named bonuses, each with its own value, that knock out the last remaining fears.
And write the boldest guarantee you can actually stand behind, tied to the specific result they want.
Sixth, name the whole thing so it points at the dream, states the timeframe, and wears an appealing container, and get ready to refresh that name when it tires.
And then, the one question, the question Hormozi wants you to hold up against everything you ever sell, forever.
Is this an offer that someone in my market would feel stupid saying no to.
Not, is it good.
Not, is it competitive.
Would they feel actually foolish walking away.
If the honest answer is yes, go sell it with total confidence, because the value is really there and you have earned the right.
And if the honest answer is no, then you already know exactly what to do, because we just walked through every lever.
Is the dream outcome big and clear enough.
Do they believe it will work for them, and if not, where is your proof and your guarantee.
Is it fast enough, and if not, where can you collapse the time.
Is it easy enough, and if not, what effort can you take off their shoulders.
Is the stacked value obviously towering over the price.
Are your scarcity, urgency, bonuses, and guarantee actually doing their job.
You do not have to guess.
You go back to the fraction, you find the weak lever, and you fix that one thing.
That is the heart of $100M Offers.

Alex Hormozi went from a nearly broke gym owner with about a thousand dollars left to building companies worth hundreds of millions, and he insists the lever underneath all of it was learning to make offers so good that saying no felt stupid.
You now hold the same lever.
Go build something people would feel foolish turning down, price it like you believe in it, guarantee it like you mean it, and watch how much easier everything downstream becomes.
Drive safe, and go make an offer they cannot compare to anything else.
